title: Weekend Adventure

story.location: Carlos and Marta’s apartment, Plaza Mayor, a café, and Mercado de San Miguel
story.scene: The rain has cleared. Carlos invites the learner to spend one more day moving through the Madrid they now know. There is no new grammar lesson and almost no new language. Six short scenes ask the learner to greet, navigate, order, shop, describe, and talk personally with less help each time.
story.mission: Complete one connected Madrid day in Spanish, from the first greeting to the final goodbye.
story.continuity: The episode pays off the promise at the end of Lesson 9 and connects all three Chapter 1 landmarks into one final journey.

emotionalArc.startsWith: The learner may still think of the previous nine lessons as separate small skills.
emotionalArc.endsWith: The learner realizes they have spent a supported day greeting, deciding, navigating, ordering, shopping, describing, and talking about themselves in Spanish.
emotionalArc.earnedBy: Completing several familiar situations as one continuous experience while Carlos gradually reduces his support.

apples.spanish: Todavía recuerdo las manzanas que elegiste. Hoy tú vuelves a decidir.
apples.english: I still remember the apples you chose. Today you make the decisions again.

bread.spanish: Todavía recuerdo el pan que elegiste. Hoy tú vuelves a decidir.
bread.english: I still remember the bread you chose. Today you make the decisions again.

cheese.spanish: Todavía recuerdo el queso que elegiste. Hoy tú vuelves a decidir.
cheese.english: I still remember the cheese you chose. Today you make the decisions again.

coffee.spanish: Todavía recuerdo el café que elegiste. Hoy tú vuelves a decidir.
coffee.english: I still remember the coffee you chose. Today you make the decisions again.

flowers.spanish: Todavía recuerdo las flores que elegiste. Hoy tú vuelves a decidir.
flowers.english: I still remember the flowers you chose. Today you make the decisions again.

tomatoes.spanish: Todavía recuerdo los tomates que elegiste. Hoy tú vuelves a decidir.
tomatoes.english: I still remember the tomatoes you chose. Today you make the decisions again.

microCliffhanger.speaker: Elena
microCliffhanger.spanish: Antes de volver a Granada, Javier y yo queremos invitarte. La próxima parte de tu viaje empieza allí.
microCliffhanger.english: Before returning to Granada, Javier and I want to invite you. The next part of your journey begins there.

carlosIntroduction.title: One more day in Madrid
carlosIntroduction.message: When we met, I guided every step. Today I’ll stay beside you, but you’ll do the communicating. We’ll revisit the places that have become familiar and connect everything you already know. Nothing needs to be perfect. Keep the day moving in Spanish.
carlosIntroduction.prompt: Carlos asks: ¿Puedes pasar un día más explorando Madrid conmigo?

miniMissions.title: Start the day
miniMissions.goal: Greet Carlos, answer a check-in, and make a simple plan.

miniMissions.title: Lead through the plaza
miniMissions.goal: Ask where the next stop is and follow three direction cues.

miniMissions.title: Order without a script
miniMissions.goal: Order one drink politely and close the exchange.

miniMissions.title: Choose at the market
miniMissions.goal: Choose an item, ask the price, and complete the purchase.

miniMissions.title: Return somewhere familiar
miniMissions.goal: Say what exists in a room and where one object is.

miniMissions.title: Tell Carlos about your day
miniMissions.goal: Describe four parts of the day and close the chapter naturally.

canDo: Carry familiar Spanish across six connected situations.
Recover with a polite repair phrase instead of switching immediately to English.
Make simple decisions about a route, an order, and a purchase.
Describe a place and a personal day with less support from Carlos.
Close a complete social day naturally.
objectives: Reuse the communication outcomes from Lessons 1–9 without a new grammar target.
Move from one scene to the next without resetting the conversation.
Complete familiar tasks with shorter prompts from Carlos.
Recognize the learner’s growth across the complete Madrid chapter.

recycledLanguage.use: Open, sustain, repair, and close the day.
Handle a brief meeting if someone new joins.
Talk naturally with Carlos and Marta at home.
Confirm the day’s plan and timing.
Order and close at the café.
Describe the apartment on returning home.
Tell Carlos how the complete day unfolded.

essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: Good morning, Carlos. How are you?

essentialPhrases.usage: Take part in deciding the plan.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: We’re ready. What do we do first?
Excuse me, where is the market?

essentialPhrases.usage: Repair the conversation without abandoning Spanish.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: More slowly, please. Can you repeat?

essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Quisiera un café con leche, por favor.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: I would like a coffee with milk, please.

essentialPhrases.usage: Ask for the price of the chosen item.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: How much does this bread cost?

essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Hay un sofá en el salón.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: There is a sofa in the living room.

essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: La foto está al lado de los libros.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: The photo is beside the books.
Afterwards, we walk to the plaza.

essentialPhrases.english: It has been a good day.
essentialPhrases.usage: Recognize and use a natural closing reflection as one fixed phrase.
essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Gracias, Carlos. Ha sido un buen día.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: Thank you, Carlos. It has been a good day.

consolidation.purpose: Integrate Chapter 1 communication outcomes inside one continuous day.
supportPlan: Carlos models the opening greeting only.
At Plaza Mayor, Carlos gives a short reminder but no translation.
At the café and market, the learner chooses and speaks first.
At the apartment, Carlos asks questions without supplying the answer.
The final reflection is completed with no model visible.

vocabulary.tip: Return from Lesson 1 and say it before Carlos prompts you.
Ask the question back instead of only answering it.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Very well. And you? How are you?

vocabulary.tip: Use it to keep the whole mission in the present day.
Reuse the fixed meeting-time chunk from Lesson 4.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: We’ll see each other at ten.

vocabulary.tip: Add the next destination after está.
Treat it as one complete route cue.
Connect the word with the matching hand.
Keep the complete phrase a la izquierda.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: La entrada está a la izquierda.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: The entrance is on the left.

vocabulary.tip: A successful learner repairs instead of pretending to understand.
Add por favor to soften the request.
Use it before the drink you choose.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I would like a coffee, please.

vocabulary.tip: A natural short way to identify your order.
Use it when the server asks ¿Algo más?
Use este with a masculine singular item.
Ask after making the item clear.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: How much does this cheese cost?

vocabulary.tip: Ask only if you need one.
Use it to introduce what exists.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Hay una foto en el salón.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: There is a photo in the living room.

vocabulary.tip: Use it to locate one known object.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: La foto está en la estantería.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: The photo is on the bookshelf.

vocabulary.tip: It works for city landmarks and household objects.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Está al lado de los libros.

vocabulary.tip: Use it to begin the final reflection.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Por la mañana, saludo a Carlos.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: In the morning, I greet Carlos.

vocabulary.tip: Use it for the middle of the day.
Connect it to the market or plaza scene.
vocabulary.exampleSpanish: Por la tarde, voy al mercado.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: In the afternoon, I go to the market.

vocabulary.tip: Use it for the final apartment scene.
Link one scene to the next.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Afterwards, we walk to the plaza.

dialogue.title: One complete day
dialogue.setting: Six short scenes connect the Chapter 1 landmarks. Carlos gives less help as the day continues.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Buenos días. ¿Puedes pasar un día más explorando Madrid conmigo?
lines.english: Good morning. Can you spend one more day exploring Madrid with me?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Buenos días, Carlos. Sí, claro. ¿Cómo estás?
lines.english: Good morning, Carlos. Yes, of course. How are you?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Muy bien. Hoy tú decides y tú hablas primero.
lines.english: Very well. Today you decide and you speak first.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Estamos en la plaza. Necesitamos encontrar el mercado.
lines.english: We are in the plaza. We need to find the market.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Perdón, ¿dónde está el mercado?
lines.english: Excuse me, where is the market?

lines.speaker: Vecino
lines.spanish: Todo recto y después a la derecha. Está al lado de una cafetería.
lines.english: Straight ahead and then to the right. It is beside a café.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Todo recto y a la derecha. Gracias por la ayuda.
lines.english: Straight ahead and to the right. Thank you for the help.

lines.speaker: Camarera
lines.spanish: Buenos días. ¿Qué quieres?
lines.english: Good morning. What would you like?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Quisiera un café con leche, por favor.
lines.english: I would like a coffee with milk, please.

lines.speaker: Camarera
lines.spanish: Claro. ¿Algo más?
lines.english: Of course. Anything else?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Nada más, gracias.
lines.english: Nothing else, thank you.

lines.speaker: Vendedor
lines.spanish: Hola. ¿Qué necesitas?
lines.english: Hello. What do you need?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Quiero este pan, por favor. ¿Cuánto cuesta?
lines.english: I want this bread, please. How much does it cost?

lines.speaker: Vendedor
lines.spanish: Cuesta tres euros.
lines.english: It costs three euros.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Perfecto. Una bolsa, por favor. Gracias.
lines.english: Perfect. A bag, please. Thank you.

lines.speaker: Marta
lines.spanish: Ya estáis aquí. ¿Qué hay en el salón?
lines.english: You’re back. What is in the living room?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Hay un sofá, una guitarra y una foto.
lines.english: There is a sofa, a guitar, and a photo.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: ¿Dónde está la foto?
lines.english: Where is the photo?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Está en la estantería, al lado de los libros.
lines.english: It is on the bookshelf, beside the books.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Cuéntame el día. ¿Qué haces por la mañana?
lines.english: Tell me about the day. What do you do in the morning?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Por la mañana, desayuno con Carlos. Después, camino por la plaza. Al mediodía, como en un café. Por la tarde, practico español en el mercado. Ahora descanso en casa.
lines.english: In the morning, I have breakfast with Carlos. Afterwards, I walk through the plaza. At midday, I eat in a café. In the afternoon, I practice Spanish at the market. Now I rest at home.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Cuando nos conocimos, sabías unas palabras. Hoy pasamos todo el día hablando español juntos.
lines.english: When we met, you knew a few words. Today we spent the whole day speaking Spanish together.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Gracias, Carlos. Ha sido un buen día. Hasta mañana.
lines.english: Thank you, Carlos. It has been a good day. See you tomorrow.

comprehension.prompt: What changes about Carlos’s role today?
options: He lets the learner speak and decide first
comprehension.answer: He lets the learner speak and decide first

options: Plaza Mayor, Mercado de San Miguel, and the apartment
comprehension.answer: Plaza Mayor, Mercado de San Miguel, and the apartment

options: The learner completes a connected day in Spanish
The learner memorizes a new tense
comprehension.answer: The learner completes a connected day in Spanish

listening.title: Madrid from morning to evening
listening.instructions: Listen, follow along, then check what you understood.
listening.naturalScript: Buenos días. Hoy pasamos un día por Madrid. Primero caminamos por Plaza Mayor y preguntamos dónde está el mercado. Después pedimos café. En el Mercado de San Miguel compramos pan. Por la tarde volvemos al apartamento. Allí hablamos de la casa y recordamos nuestro día. Ha sido un buen día.
listening.slowScript: Buenos días. / Hoy pasamos un día por Madrid. / Primero, / caminamos por Plaza Mayor / y preguntamos / dónde está el mercado. / Después, / pedimos café. / En el Mercado de San Miguel / compramos pan. / Por la tarde, / volvemos al apartamento. / Allí, / hablamos de la casa / y recordamos nuestro día. / Ha sido un buen día.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.spanish: Buenos días. Hoy pasamos un día por Madrid.
transcript.english: Good morning. Today we are spending a day around Madrid.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.spanish: Primero caminamos por Plaza Mayor y preguntamos dónde está el mercado.
transcript.english: First we walk through Plaza Mayor and ask where the market is.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.spanish: En el Mercado de San Miguel compramos pan.
transcript.english: At Mercado de San Miguel we buy bread.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.spanish: Por la tarde volvemos al apartamento.
transcript.english: In the afternoon we return to the apartment.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.spanish: Allí hablamos de la casa y recordamos nuestro día.
transcript.english: There we talk about the home and remember our day.

transcript.speaker: Learner
transcript.english: It has been a good day.

comprehension.prompt: Where does the connected day begin?
comprehension.explanation: The first destination named is Plaza Mayor.

comprehension.prompt: What happens after asking for directions?
comprehension.explanation: Después pedimos café means afterwards we order coffee.

comprehension.prompt: What do they buy at Mercado de San Miguel?
comprehension.explanation: Compramos pan means we buy bread.
In the afternoon they return to the familiar apartment and reflect on the day.

options: First we walk through Plaza Mayor.
First we return to the apartment.
items.answer: First we walk through Plaza Mayor.

options: At the market we buy bread.
At the market we drink tea.
At the market we find the apartment.
items.answer: At the market we buy bread.

items.spanish: Por la tarde volvemos al apartamento.
options: In the afternoon we return to the apartment.
In the morning we leave Madrid.
At night we go to the airport.
items.answer: In the afternoon we return to the apartment.

options: It has been a good day.
items.answer: It has been a good day.

pronunciation.focus: Clear mission phrases under light pressure
tips: Pause after Perdón before asking a stranger for help.
Keep derecha and izquierda distinct; clarity matters more than speed.
Use a calm polite rhythm in quisiera and por favor.
When you need repair, say ¿Puede repetir? confidently rather than apologetically.

items.meaning: Excuse me, where is the market?
I would like a coffee with milk.
items.note: Keep quisiera flowing as one word.
Stress cuánto and keep the question short.

items.meaning: It has been a good day.
items.note: Treat this as one closing phrase; the h is silent.

speaking.instructions: Carlos gives you less help as you connect the whole Madrid day one scene at a time.

items.label: Listen
items.cue: Open the day warmly and ask the question back.
items.meaning: Good morning, Carlos. How are you?

items.label: Your turn
items.carlosPrompt: Hoy tú decides y tú hablas primero.
items.cue: Use the repair phrase you can rely on if anything becomes unclear.
items.prompt: Ask Carlos to repeat more slowly.
items.text: Perdón, ¿puede repetir más despacio, por favor?
items.meaning: Excuse me, can you repeat more slowly, please?
items.focus: Say it confidently enough to use in a real exchange.

items.label: Keep talking
items.cue: Ask where it is, confirm one route cue, and thank the speaker.
items.text: ¿Dónde está el mercado? Todo recto. Gracias.
items.meaning: Where is the market? Straight ahead. Thank you.
items.focus: Keep the exchange moving with short phrases.

items.label: One more challenge
items.carlosPrompt: Estamos en la cafetería. Tú eliges.
items.cue: Order your chosen drink, answer ¿Algo más?, and close.
items.text: Quisiera un café, por favor. Nada más, gracias.
items.meaning: I would like a coffee, please. Nothing else, thank you.
items.focus: Choose your own drink when you perform it.

items.label: Finish the conversation
items.cue: Connect the plaza, café, market, apartment, and goodbye in your own words.
items.prompt: Tell the complete day without a full model.
items.text: Por la mañana... Después... Al mediodía... Por la tarde... Al final...
items.meaning: In the morning... Afterwards... At midday... In the afternoon... Finally...
items.focus: Use the prompts only as anchors; supply the actions yourself.

flashcards.selectionRule: Only high-value Chapter 1 survival chunks appear. No new vocabulary is introduced in the review deck.

quizIntroduction: Follow the connected Madrid day. Choose the response that keeps each real situation moving.

quiz.prompt: Carlos says Buenos días. What keeps the greeting going?
quiz.explanation: Return the greeting and ask the check-in back.

quiz.prompt: You need the market. Which question should you ask?
quiz.explanation: Use ¿Dónde está...? for the location of one place.
Todo recto is the fixed direction cue for straight ahead.

quiz.prompt: Which phrase politely begins a drink order?
quiz.explanation: Quisiera plus the item makes a polite café order.

quiz.prompt: The server asks ¿Algo más? You are finished. Say...
quiz.explanation: Nada más clearly closes the order.

quiz.prompt: At the market, which exchange is complete?
quiz.explanation: It identifies the item and asks its price.

quiz.prompt: You missed a direction. What is the strongest response?
quiz.explanation: Repairing the exchange is a communication success, not a failure.

quiz.prompt: Marta asks ¿Qué hay en el salón? Answer naturally.
options: Hay un sofá y una guitarra.
Quiero un salón a las diez.
quiz.answer: Hay un sofá y una guitarra.
quiz.explanation: Hay introduces what exists in the room.

quiz.prompt: Carlos asks where the photo is. Answer naturally.
quiz.explanation: Use está to locate the known photo.

quiz.prompt: Which line puts the day in order?
options: Primero la plaza. Después el café. Al final, casa.
quiz.answer: Primero la plaza. Después el café. Al final, casa.
quiz.explanation: The connectors establish a clear beginning, middle, and end.

quiz.prompt: Translate: Por la tarde volvemos a casa.
options: In the afternoon we return home.
In the morning we go to the market.
At midday we ask for directions.
quiz.answer: In the afternoon we return home.
quiz.explanation: Por la tarde means in the afternoon and volvemos a casa means we return home.

quiz.prompt: Translate: Ha sido un buen día.
options: It has been a good day.
quiz.answer: It has been a good day.
quiz.explanation: Learn this as a natural closing reflection for the chapter.

quiz.prompt: Which response connects three earlier lessons correctly?
options: ¿Dónde está el café? Quisiera un café, por favor. Nada más, gracias.
quiz.answer: ¿Dónde está el café? Quisiera un café, por favor. Nada más, gracias.
quiz.explanation: It combines directions, ordering, and a natural closing without mixing the patterns.

quiz.prompt: A vendor says Cuesta tres euros. What should you demonstrate?
options: Understand the price and complete or decline the purchase.
quiz.answer: Understand the price and complete or decline the purchase.
quiz.explanation: The capstone measures whether the learner can act on familiar language in context.

quiz.prompt: What shows growing independence most clearly?
options: Using a repair phrase and continuing in Spanish
Waiting for Carlos to translate every line
quiz.answer: Using a repair phrase and continuing in Spanish
quiz.explanation: Independent communication includes recovering when understanding is incomplete.

options: Gracias por el día. Ha sido un buen día. Hasta mañana.
Hay un adiós a la derecha.
quiz.answer: Gracias por el día. Ha sido un buen día. Hasta mañana.
quiz.explanation: It reflects on the day, thanks Carlos, and closes naturally.

quiz.prompt: The learner forgets one word at the market. What should Carlos correct first?
options: Only the error that blocks the purchase
Every accent from the whole conversation
Nothing, even if meaning is lost
quiz.answer: Only the error that blocks the purchase
quiz.explanation: Habla prioritizes successful communication, then one useful correction.

quiz.prompt: Which performance completes the chapter mission?
options: Greet, navigate, order, shop, describe the apartment, recount the day, and say goodbye in connected Spanish.
quiz.answer: Greet, navigate, order, shop, describe the apartment, recount the day, and say goodbye in connected Spanish.
quiz.explanation: The finale proves the learner can connect Chapter 1 skills across a complete social day.

miniConversation.title: The day without a script
miniConversation.setting: Carlos gives only short scene prompts. The learner chooses the details and keeps the journey moving.
miniConversation.goal: Complete the six mini-missions with no full model answer visible.

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.spanish: Buenos días. Sí, estoy listo. ¿Qué hacemos primero?
turns.english: Good morning. Yes, I’m ready. What do we do first?

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.spanish: Primero, la plaza. Después, tú eliges.
turns.english: First, the plaza. Afterwards, you choose.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.english: We’re already here. What would you like to drink?

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.spanish: Quisiera un café, por favor. Nada más, gracias.
turns.english: I would like a coffee, please. Nothing else, thank you.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.english: Now we’re going to the market. What do you buy?

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.english: I want this bread. How much does it cost?

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.spanish: Volvemos a casa. ¿Qué hay en el salón?
turns.english: We return home. What is in the living room?

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.spanish: Hay un sofá y una foto. La foto está al lado de los libros.
turns.english: There is a sofa and a photo. The photo is beside the books.

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.spanish: Por la mañana, caminamos. Después, tomamos café. Por la tarde, compramos pan. Al final, descansamos en casa.
turns.english: In the morning, we walk. Afterwards, we have coffee. In the afternoon, we buy bread. Finally, we rest at home.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.spanish: Excelente. Hoy has vivido Madrid en español.
turns.english: Excellent. Today you lived Madrid in Spanish.

culture.title: A day together is flexible
culture.text: A social day in Madrid may move between walking, coffee, shopping, and time at home without a rigid schedule. Plans still vary by person and family. Confirm the important meeting time, stay flexible about smaller details, and use messages if the plan changes. The goal is not to imitate one ‘Spanish lifestyle’ but to communicate comfortably while the day unfolds.

worldBuilding.carlosSpanish: A esta hora la plaza está más tranquila. Dentro de poco habrá mucha más gente.
worldBuilding.carlosEnglish: At this hour the plaza is quieter. Before long there will be many more people.

worldBuilding.moment: returning to Mercado de San Miguel
worldBuilding.carlosSpanish: La primera vez vine para ayudarte. Hoy ya sabes qué preguntar tú solo.
worldBuilding.carlosEnglish: The first time I came to help you. Today you already know what to ask on your own.

worldBuilding.carlosSpanish: Desde aquí hemos visto lluvia, sol y muchas conversaciones. Madrid ya no parece tan desconocido.
worldBuilding.carlosEnglish: From here we have seen rain, sun, and many conversations. Madrid no longer feels so unfamiliar.

livingWorldInteractions.title: A postcard from Granada
livingWorldInteractions.prompt: A postcard at a Plaza Mayor stand shows the Alhambra glowing at sunset. Look closer.
livingWorldInteractions.carlosSpanish: Granada es la ciudad de mi familia. Mi madre siempre dice que tengo que enseñártela algún día.
livingWorldInteractions.carlosEnglish: Granada is my family’s city. My mother always says I have to show it to you one day.

nativeSpeech.note: A natural way to begin the next part of an outing.

nativeSpeech.meaning: Do you feel like having a coffee?
nativeSpeech.note: Recognize this common Spain invitation; the formal grammar is not required yet.

nativeSpeech.meaning: It has been a good day.
nativeSpeech.note: Use the whole expression as a closing chunk without analyzing the tense.

commonMistakes.mistake: Trying to make every sentence complex
commonMistakes.better: Use short complete phrases and connect them.
commonMistakes.why: A connected A1 conversation is stronger than one advanced sentence the learner cannot control.

commonMistakes.mistake: Switching to English after one missed word
commonMistakes.better: Use ¿Puede repetir? or Más despacio, por favor.
commonMistakes.why: Repair phrases keep the interaction alive.

commonMistakes.better: Introduce with hay; locate the known object with está.
commonMistakes.why: The two questions are different: what exists versus where it is.

commonMistakes.mistake: Waiting for Carlos to answer first
commonMistakes.better: Use the familiar opener before Carlos supplies a model.
commonMistakes.why: The finale measures growing independence, not perfect recall.

realLifeMission.title: Chapter Finale: One Day in Madrid
realLifeMission.mission: Complete all six mini-missions as one connected day. Carlos may repeat or slow down, but he does not translate or supply the learner’s decisions.
successCriteria: Greet Carlos and ask a question back.
Ask for one destination and follow the route.
Order one chosen drink and close the exchange.
Choose a market item, ask the price, and complete the purchase.
Say what exists in the apartment and locate one object.
Describe the day in order using time phrases and connectors.
Use one repair phrase if communication breaks down.
Close naturally with thanks and a goodbye.
realLifeMission.completionResponse: When we met, you knew a few words. Today we spent the whole day speaking Spanish together. You did not need perfect Spanish—you kept communicating. That is real progress.
realLifeMission.correctionRule: Never interrupt a successful mini-mission for a minor error. At each scene transition, correct at most one issue that blocked meaning or would cause a real-world problem.
realLifeMission.suggestedPartner: Carlos, Marta, and familiar Madrid speakers
realLifeMission.handoff: Elena and Javier invite the learner to continue the journey with the family in Granada.

canDo: I can begin and end a social day naturally.
I can navigate, order, and shop with familiar A1 Spanish.
I can describe a place and talk about my day.
I can repair a conversation instead of giving up.
I can connect several simple situations into one experience.
selfCheck: Did I speak first in at least three scenes?
Did I make choices rather than repeat only Carlos’s words?
Did I recover when I missed something?
Could another Spanish speaker understand the goal in every scene?
Do the three Madrid landmarks now feel familiar?

chapterCeremony.title: Your first chapter is complete
chapterCeremony.subtitle: Nine lessons became one complete day. Your Madrid journey now has a beginning, a middle, and a memory.
chapterCeremony.carlosSpanish: Cuando nos conocimos, sabías unas palabras. Hoy pasamos todo el día hablando español juntos. Estoy muy orgulloso de ti.
chapterCeremony.carlosEnglish: When we met, you knew a few words. Today we spent the whole day speaking Spanish together. I’m very proud of you.

passportStamp.title: Madrid Weekend
passportStamp.description: You completed a connected day across the familiar places and conversations of Chapter 1.
passportStamp.unlockCondition: Complete all six mini-missions in Lesson 10.

achievement.title: Madrid Ready
achievement.description: Complete the Madrid chapter as one connected Spanish experience.

carlosClosing: When we met, you knew only a few words. Today you greeted people, found your way, ordered, shopped, described my home, and told the story of our day in Spanish. I’m proud of you—your Madrid chapter is complete, and my family is waiting for us in Granada.
